title: The Bad Ones: O(n²), O(2ⁿ), O(n!)
subtitle: When code grinds to a halt — and how to recognize it

sections.heading: Knowing what to avoid
sections.body:
You don't need to memorize every Big O class — but recognizing the BAD shapes saves you from accidentally writing them.

In this lesson we'll look at:

* **O(n²)** — quadratic. Two nested loops over the same data.
* **O(n³)** — cubic. Three nested loops. Often a sign you should use a smarter algorithm.
* **O(2ⁿ)** — exponential. Brute-force search over all subsets, or naive recursion that branches twice.
* **O(n!)** — factorial. Permutations. The traveling salesman in its purest, dumbest form.

All four are bad, but they're bad in different ways.

sections.heading: Spotting nested-loop complexity
sections.body:
When you see nested loops, count them — but be careful:

* Two loops, both up to n → **O(n²)**
* Three loops, all up to n → **O(n³)**
* Loops that range over different things multiply: a loop over an array of length n inside a loop over a string of length m → **O(n·m)**
* A loop that **halves** its range each step is **O(log n)**, NOT O(n)
* Two loops in *sequence* (not nested) add, but the bigger one wins: O(n) + O(n²) = O(n²)

sections.caption: The fib(5) call tree — see how much recomputation happens

data.text:
fib(5)
                      /      \
                fib(4)        fib(3)
               /     \        /    \
           fib(3)  fib(2)  fib(2)  fib(1)
           /   \    / \     / \
        fib(2) f(1) f(1) f(0) f(1) f(0)
        /   \
      f(1) f(0)

  fib(3) is computed twice. fib(2) three times. fib(1) five times. fib(0) three times.
  At n=30, the same subproblems are recomputed millions of times.

  Fix: MEMOIZE (cache the result of each fib(k)). Brings it down to O(n) time.

sections.heading: Why O(2ⁿ) is a death sentence
sections.body:
Exponential growth is the most punishing common complexity. Look at the numbers:

* n = 10 → 2¹⁰ = 1,024 operations (fine)
* n = 30 → 2³⁰ ≈ a billion (slow)
* n = 50 → 2⁵⁰ ≈ a quadrillion (hours)
* n = 100 → 2¹⁰⁰ ≈ more atoms than there are in the observable universe

If you see exponential behavior on a problem, **stop**. Look for:

* **Memoization** — caching subproblem results (dynamic programming)
* **Pruning** — skipping branches that can't possibly improve the answer
* **Mathematical shortcut** — closed-form formula instead of brute force

sections.heading: When are O(n²), O(2ⁿ), O(n!) acceptable?
sections.body:
Not all bad complexities are bugs — sometimes the problem itself is hard.

* **O(n²)** is fine for small n (a few thousand). Bubble sort and insertion sort are great for n ≤ ~50, and Timsort actually uses insertion sort for small subarrays.

* **O(2ⁿ)** is sometimes necessary. Generating every subset of a 20-element set genuinely needs 2²⁰ ≈ 1M operations. Some NP-hard problems have no known polynomial-time algorithm.

* **O(n!)** is rarely 'acceptable' but sometimes 'the only known approach' (e.g. brute-force TSP, scheduling problems). For n ≤ 10 it's fine.

The key: **know what you're getting into**. Bad complexity on large input is a bug. Bad complexity on tiny input is engineering.

questions.question: Which is fastest-growing?
questions.explanation: n! > 2ⁿ > n² > n log n, for sufficiently large n.

questions.question: What's the complexity of this function?
questions.explanation: Inner loop runs 0+1+2+...+(n-1) = n(n-1)/2 times → still O(n²).

questions.question: Naive recursive Fibonacci is O(2ⁿ). What technique brings it down to O(n)?
questions.explanation: Memoization stores each fib(k) the first time we compute it. Subsequent calls are O(1) lookups.

questions.question: For which n is brute-force O(n!) reasonable?
questions.explanation: 10! = 3.6 million — still fast. 13! starts to push 6 billion. After ~12, O(n!) is impractical.
